video_title: Everything You Know About Investing Comes From One Weird Era
gist: Historian Joseph Moore (author of 'How to Get Rich in American History') argues with Meb Faber that nearly all modern investing advice — stocks-for-the-long-run, FIRE, gold-bugging — was invented in the 1920s as a desperate response to newly-arrived inflation and income tax, and that most 'timeless' rules (real estate always rises, stocks always beat bonds, dividends are free money) are artifacts of one weird era. They connect this to crypto's likely future in dollar-backed stablecoins and to why beating the market rarely pays.

themes.theme: Money as a perishable asset (pre-1860s currency)

insights.claim: In the 1800s the US federal government did not print money; currency was self-issued by banks, companies and even individuals and held value only so long as the issuer could redeem it, so savers who held cash were routinely wiped out when the issuer failed or moved west.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: The pre-greenback era is a historical template for a world of competing private monies — relevant framing for stablecoin and crypto-issuance debates.
insights.quote: money was issued on the authority of the issuer

insights.claim: Historical American financial advice was the opposite of 'save': grandparents told heirs to never hold cash but spend it immediately into tangible assets (real estate, tools, a business) because self-issued currency could go to zero overnight, and immigrant families who hoarded cash were the ones who got wiped out.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Explains the deep cultural bias toward hard assets in inflationary regimes — a bias that recurs today in high-inflation emerging markets.
insights.quote: Now I want you to understand, grandson, never save money.

insights.claim: The greenback dollar introduced during the Civil War let Americans hold savable, value-stable money for the first time, and they abandoned self-issued peer-to-peer currency in the 1860s.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: A historical precedent that populations rapidly ditch volatile private money once a stable, redeemable alternative appears.

insights.claim: Moore predicts crypto's durable use case will not be Bitcoin itself but dollar-backed stablecoins backed by US Treasury bills, letting people in inflationary economies transact globally in dollar value the way 1950s-70s Eurodollars did.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Bullish structurally for Treasury-collateralized stablecoins and for T-bill demand; skeptical that Bitcoin is the end-state payment rail.
insights.quote: Bitcoin kind of showed the way that, you know, kind of like Zeppelin showed you could fly, but it's not the best way to go to grandma's.

insights.claim: Moore argues Bitcoin — founded to undermine the fiat regime — will ironically end up its greatest support, because Treasury-backed stablecoins built on crypto rails extend global demand for and use of the US dollar.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Reframes stablecoin adoption as dollar-hegemony reinforcement rather than a threat — relevant to policy and to dollar/Treasury demand theses.
insights.quote: an ideological project in Bitcoin that was founded to undermine the fiat currency regime is going to end up being its greatest support

themes.theme: Real estate: modest fortunes, not great ones

insights.claim: Zero of the 100 largest US fortunes were made in real estate; the Forbes 500 wealthy typically build wealth in operating businesses and put money into real estate only afterward.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Counters the folk belief that real estate is the path to great wealth; concentration in a business, not property, is where the largest fortunes come from.
insights.quote: Real estate is a great way to build a modest fortune. It is not the way to build a great fortune

insights.claim: US residential real estate did not appreciate for most of American history: a home in Pittsburgh, Atlanta or Houston cost the same inflation-adjusted in the 1990s as in the 1890s (a St. Louis Federal Reserve statistic).
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
entities: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
insights.so_what: The 'real estate always goes up' premise is a recent regime feature, not a law — a warning against extrapolating post-1970s home appreciation.
insights.quote: A residential home in Pittsburgh, in Atlanta, in Houston, in most American cities cost the same inflation adjusted in the 1990s as it had in the 1890s.

insights.claim: Real estate's real edge historically was leverage — the ability to borrow ~95 cents on the dollar against an asset that holds value — not appreciation, a financing deal unavailable in any other asset for ordinary families.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Reframes housing returns as a leverage/financing story, so returns are highly sensitive to rate and credit availability, not intrinsic asset growth.
insights.quote: Where is the other place that you could leverage 95 cents on the dollar like the Chicago Board of Trade?

insights.claim: Meb Faber argues real estate builds wealth mainly because a mortgage is forced, systematic saving of money owners would otherwise spend — the same mechanism that makes automatic 401(k)s and target-date funds work — rather than because of superior returns.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Implies the 'return' on housing is partly a behavioral commitment device; passive index auto-investing can capture the same discipline benefit.

insights.claim: The 1948 US housing shortage was statistically about twice the size of today's shortage (units versus people needing homes), and that post-WWII scarcity plus rising inflation fueled the little/zero-money-down real estate seminar boom of the 1950s-70s.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Historical analogue for reading today's housing shortage and the speculative behavior scarcity plus inflation can induce.

insights.claim: Moore says real estate delivers active (above-passive) returns only when run like an operating business; treated purely as an asset class it yields low, bond-like passive returns.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Sets expectations for property investors: outsized returns require operational work, not just ownership — relevant to REIT vs. direct-ownership choices.
insights.quote: you want a bond with an HVAC

insights.claim: Moore's post-2008 thesis was that distressed real estate in areas with population pressure would not go to zero and would recover, on the reasoning that a second 2008-scale crash would not immediately follow the first; he built a 40+ property portfolio starting around 2013.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: A worked example of buying distressed assets after a crash when population demand underpins them and repeat-crash odds are low.
insights.quote: we weren't going to have another 2008 right after 2008

insights.claim: New academic databases show stocks did NOT always beat bonds long-term: bonds beat stocks through the 19th century and the two were roughly tied until about World War II.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Undercuts the equity-risk-premium-is-permanent assumption underlying stocks-for-the-long-run allocation defaults.
insights.quote: Stocks did not always beat bonds in the long term.

insights.claim: For most of American history ordinary people could not own a stock index — replicating it required 100 shares of every constituent, over $1 million then (roughly $12-20 million today) — so average Americans speculated on price moves in 'bucket shops' rather than owning equities.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: The mass equity-ownership era is very recent; low-cost index access is a structural change that reshaped who holds stocks and how markets behave.

insights.claim: Meb Faber notes stocks and the long bond delivered the same return over roughly a 40-year window measured to the COVID low, echoing a 60-70 year 19th-century stretch where stocks underperformed bonds.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Multi-decade equity underperformance versus bonds is not hypothetical — supports genuine diversification across stocks and long-duration bonds.

themes.theme: The 1920s inflation-and-tax regime that birthed modern advice

insights.claim: US consumer prices were flat for a full century — a gallon of milk cost 24 cents in both 1812 and 1912 — then, driven by World War I and Federal Reserve actions, prices doubled in about five years, destroying every investment assumption that had held for 100 years.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Shows how fast a stable-price regime can flip, invalidating 'timeless' rules — the core historical case for planning around regime change.
insights.quote: For a hundred years, prices did not rise.

insights.claim: Moore's central thesis: essentially all modern investing advice — 'stocks for the long run,' the first FIRE book (1919), and the first gold bugs — emerged in the 1920s as a desperate scramble to outrun two brand-new forces, inflation and the income tax.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: If today's canon is contingent on a specific inflation/tax regime, a regime change could invalidate it — the episode's headline edge.
insights.quote: Everything that you think of about financial advice today all comes in this desperate attempt to beat inflation and beat the income tax, which are both brand new.

insights.claim: Meb Faber cites an Idea Farm stat that not a single country has averaged 2% inflation or less since 1971, arguing the modern fiat era forces investors to own real, productive assets to escape a persistent inflation tax.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Supports a structural tilt toward real assets and away from holding cash in any fiat currency over long horizons.

themes.theme: Why beating the market rarely pays

insights.claim: Moore argues beating the market is possible but not worth it for ordinary investors: managers running $600 million or more tend to beat by only about half a percent working 70-hour weeks with Yale-level training, and applied to a median 401(k) balance that edge is only about $300-500 a year.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Quantifies the poor time-ROI of active management for small accounts — a case for indexing on effort-adjusted grounds, not just fees.
insights.quote: the people who do beat the market, if they control $600 million or more, they tend to beat the market by one half of 1%

insights.claim: Moore reframes the real benchmark as beating inflation and taxes and one's own life goals rather than an index, arguing the S&P comparison is the wrong game for most investors.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Shifts the objective function from relative return to after-inflation, after-tax goal attainment — changes portfolio and behavior design.
insights.quote: You want to use the market to beat the goals you have in your own life, not some arbitrary number created by the benchmark.

insights.claim: Moore cites the documented 'Cramer bounce': under strict criteria, a stock Jim Cramer recommends on Mad Money rises for roughly 50-55 days and can then be profitably shorted as it reverts; he traded it and beat the market gross, though net of fees the edge was different.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: A named, testable short-term mean-reversion anomaly around televised stock recommendations.

insights.claim: From the George Washington administration until Michael Jackson's Thriller album, dividends were 96% of US stock returns; since then returns have been almost entirely price appreciation — so the market rewards investors in a fundamentally different way than it did historically.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Historical total-return data dominated by dividends can mislead when applied to today's appreciation-driven, low-yield market.
insights.quote: From the George Washington administration until Michael Jackson's Thriller album, dividends were 96% of returns.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 dividend yield is about 1.06% and on the verge of dipping below 1% for the first time ever, an all-time low.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Record-low equity income yield undercuts dividend-focused income strategies and signals how appreciation-dependent current equity returns are.
insights.quote: for the first time ever it's on the verge of dipping below 1%

insights.claim: Meb Faber says a commissioned survey found over 70% of retail investors — and a large, non-zero share of professionals — wrongly believe dividends are free money that does not lower the stock price, treating them like a bond coupon, a misunderstanding parts of the industry knowingly exploit in income-product and private-real-estate marketing.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Flags predatory high-yield income and private real estate marketing (e.g., '17% dividend yield') as exploiting a widespread investor misconception.
insights.quote: 70% plus of investors retail think the dividends are free money and the stock doesn't go down.

insights.claim: Meb Faber notes that capturing the historical 8-10% equity return requires reinvesting dividends, and that spending dividends breaks the compounding most people assume they will get.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Income-oriented retirees who spend dividends should not expect historical total-return figures — a core retirement-planning correction.
insights.quote: if you're spending, it doesn't compound. And if it's compounding, you can't spend it.

insights.claim: Meb Faber criticizes Michael Saylor for touting his Bitcoin-focused company's 'effective yield' with claims of ~1% volatility and a Sharpe ratio of 4, saying the figures use yield instead of performance and a 30-day window, misapplying the Sharpe ratio's definition.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A pointed warning that a prominent Bitcoin-treasury promoter's risk-adjusted-return claims are constructed from misleading inputs.

insights.claim: Moore's first wealth principle from 300 years of advice: solving other people's problems, not managing your own (lattes, budgeting), is the actual path to getting rich — most personal-finance advice only keeps you from going broke.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Reorients wealth-building (and career) strategy toward creating value for others rather than optimizing personal frugality.
insights.quote: Most of the real money is found solving someone else's problems

insights.claim: Moore argues Americans should take more risk because this is the least risky age in history — spouses and homes are insurable and bankruptcy is recoverable — noting that in the 1840s bankruptcy window, nearly half of those allowed to declare it bounced back to financial success.
insights.speaker: Joseph Moore
insights.so_what: Argues the modern risk/safety asymmetry is underexploited — a case for entrepreneurial and concentrated bets while downside is cushioned.
insights.quote: We have never lived in a less risky age.
